title: Functional Assessments: The Complete Clinical Guide
description: A comprehensive guide to functional assessments for falls prevention: TUG, sit-to-stand, gait speed, grip strength and more. Protocols, interpretation, and objective capture.
author: Able Care Clinical Team
excerpt: Functional assessments measure a person's ability to perform the physical tasks that matter for independent living. This guide covers the key tests, what they measure, how to interpret them and how objective digital capture is changing the way they are delivered.
content:
What are functional assessments?

Functional assessments measure a person's ability to perform the physical tasks that underpin independent living: standing up from a chair, walking across a room, maintaining balance, gripping objects. They are the clinical tools that translate physical capacity into actionable risk stratification.

In falls prevention, functional assessments are the frontline. They identify who is declining, how fast and in which domains, before a fall occurs. The 2022 World Guidelines for Falls Prevention and the CDC STEADI framework both recommend multifactorial functional assessment as the foundation of any falls prevention program.

The key functional assessments

Timed Up and Go (TUG)

The TUG is the most widely used clinical falls risk screen in the world. The patient rises from a seated position, walks three meters, turns, walks back and sits down. The time is recorded in seconds.

  • What it measures: Mobility, dynamic balance and transfer ability in a single composite measure.
  • Interpretation: A time above 12 seconds suggests elevated falls risk. Above 20 seconds indicates significant mobility impairment.
  • Strengths: Fast, simple, well-validated, requires no specialist equipment.
  • Limitations: It is a single composite measure. It does not isolate which domain (strength, balance, gait) is driving the result.

30-Second Chair to Stand (sit-to-stand)

The patient stands up from a standard-height chair and sits back down as many times as possible in 30 seconds, without using their arms for support.

  • What it measures: Lower-limb power and endurance. A proxy for the strength needed to get out of bed, rise from a toilet and climb stairs.
  • Interpretation: Age- and sex-matched normative data is available. Scores below the normative range indicate elevated falls risk.
  • Strengths: Directly relevant to daily function. Sensitive to change over time.
  • Limitations: Requires motivation and effort from the patient. May not be suitable for individuals with severe knee or hip pain.

4-Meter Gait Speed

The patient walks four meters at their usual pace. The time is recorded and converted to meters per second.

  • What it measures: Functional locomotion. Often called the 'sixth vital sign' for older adults.
  • Interpretation: A gait speed below 0.8 m/s is associated with increased risk of falls, hospitalization, disability and mortality. Below 0.6 m/s indicates significant functional impairment.
  • Strengths: One of the strongest single predictors of adverse health outcomes in older adults. Simple to administer.
  • Limitations: Requires a clear, flat four-meter walkway. Environmental factors can influence results.

Grip Strength

The patient squeezes a dynamometer with maximal force. The peak reading is recorded in kilograms.

  • What it measures: Upper-body strength proxy and validated marker of whole-body muscle strength.
  • Interpretation: The EWGSOP2 defines low grip strength as below 27 kg for men and below 16 kg for women. Low grip strength is independently associated with falls, frailty, sarcopenia and all-cause mortality.
  • Strengths: Quick (under 30 seconds), non-invasive, highly predictive. The Lancet (2015) found it predicts all-cause mortality more reliably than blood pressure.
  • Limitations: Requires a calibrated dynamometer. Analogue devices (e.g. Jamar) provide no digital data capture.

Why multifactorial assessment matters

Each functional assessment captures a different domain of physical function. Grip strength measures upper-body strength. Sit-to-stand measures lower-limb power. Gait speed measures locomotion. TUG captures a composite of mobility, balance and transfers.

No single test captures the full picture. The 2022 World Guidelines, CDC STEADI and NICE 2025 all recommend combining multiple measures for the most predictive screening. Research published in the BMJ (2022) found that multifactorial assessment identified significantly more at-risk older adults than any single screening tool.

Traditional vs objective capture

Traditionally, functional assessments are administered manually by a clinician using a stopwatch, a tape measure and a paper form. This approach works, but it introduces variability (who is timing, how precisely, where is the result recorded) and creates no structured data trail.

Objective digital capture changes this. Platforms like Able Assess guide any staff member through a standardized protocol, capture results automatically, score against normative data and integrate with care plans and EHR systems. The result is consistent, defensible data at every site.

Who can deliver functional assessments?

With the right technology, functional assessments do not require clinical staff. Able Assess was built so that non-clinical care workers, wellness coordinators and support staff can deliver a standardized, four-metric screening in under five minutes. This is critical for operators facing staffing constraints and needing to screen at scale.

Key takeaways

  • Functional assessments are the frontline of falls prevention, identifying decline before a fall occurs.
  • TUG, sit-to-stand, gait speed and grip strength each capture a different domain of physical function.
  • Multifactorial assessment is more predictive than any single test.
  • Digital capture eliminates variability and creates defensible, longitudinal data.
  • Non-clinical staff can deliver standardized functional assessments with the right technology.

blocks.headline: Functional assessments: the complete clinical guide.
blocks.subtitle: TUG, sit-to-stand, gait speed, grip and more. Protocols, interpretation, and objective capture.

blocks.body:
What are functional assessments?

Functional assessments measure a person's ability to perform the physical tasks that underpin independent living: standing up from a chair, walking across a room, maintaining balance, gripping objects. They are the clinical tools that translate physical capacity into actionable risk stratification.

In falls prevention, functional assessments are the frontline. They identify who is declining, how fast and in which domains, before a fall occurs. The 2022 World Guidelines for Falls Prevention and the CDC STEADI framework both recommend multifactorial functional assessment as the foundation of any falls prevention program.

The key functional assessments

Timed Up and Go (TUG)

The patient rises from a seated position, walks three meters, turns, walks back and sits down. A time above 12 seconds suggests elevated falls risk. The TUG is the most widely used clinical falls risk screen in the world.

30-Second Chair to Stand

The patient stands and sits as many times as possible in 30 seconds without using their arms. This measures lower-limb power and endurance, directly relevant to daily function like getting out of bed or climbing stairs.

4-Meter Gait Speed

The patient walks four meters at their usual pace. A gait speed below 0.8 m/s is associated with increased risk of falls, hospitalization and mortality. Often called the 'sixth vital sign' for older adults.

Grip Strength

The patient squeezes a dynamometer with maximal force. Low grip strength (below 27 kg for men, below 16 kg for women per EWGSOP2) is independently associated with falls, frailty, sarcopenia and all-cause mortality.

Why multifactorial assessment matters

Each test captures a different domain. No single test captures the full picture. The 2022 World Guidelines, CDC STEADI and NICE 2025 all recommend combining multiple measures. Research published in the BMJ (2022) confirmed that multifactorial assessment identifies significantly more at-risk individuals than any single test.

Traditional vs objective capture

Manual administration with a stopwatch and paper form introduces variability and creates no structured data trail. Digital platforms like Able Assess standardize the protocol, capture results automatically, score against normative data and integrate with care plans and EHR systems.

Who can deliver functional assessments?

With the right technology, non-clinical staff can deliver standardized functional assessments. Able Assess was built so that care workers, wellness coordinators and support staff can run a four-metric screening in under five minutes with no clinical qualification required.

blocks.heading: Four metrics. One complete picture of function.

metrics.name: Grip strength
metrics.whatItMeasures: Upper-body strength proxy captured by the GripAble sensor.
metrics.whyItMatters: Predicts all-cause mortality more reliably than systolic blood pressure. A validated marker of frailty, sarcopenia and falls risk.

metrics.name: Sit-to-stand
metrics.whatItMeasures: Lower-limb power and balance. Timed five-repetition protocol.
metrics.whyItMatters: Independently predicts falls in older adults. Captures functional decline that grip strength alone can miss.

metrics.name: Gait speed
metrics.whatItMeasures: Walking speed over a standardized distance.
metrics.whyItMatters: Known as the 'sixth vital sign' for older adults. A strong predictor of hospitalization, disability and mortality.

metrics.name: Timed Up and Go
metrics.whatItMeasures: Time to rise from a chair, walk three meters, turn, walk back and sit down.
metrics.whyItMatters: The most widely used clinical falls risk screen. Captures mobility, balance and transfer ability in a single measure.

blocks.heading: How the Able Assess workflow works

steps.title: Capture
steps.description: Run the five-minute Able Assess workflow on any tablet. The app guides staff through each test with on-screen prompts. No clinical training required.

steps.title: Score
steps.description: Results are scored instantly against age- and sex-matched normative data. Risk banding is automatic: low, intermediate or high.

steps.title: Act
steps.description: Care plan triggers and alerts are routed to the right team automatically. High-risk results prompt immediate clinical review.

steps.title: Track
steps.description: Longitudinal trends surface decline early, so you can intervene before a fall. Population-level dashboards show outcomes across sites.

blocks.heading: See the four-metric workflow in action.
blocks.bodyText: A 20-minute demo with a clinician who has run it in the field.
